point_001: The discussion comparing findings to Gordon et al. (EMNLP 2021), which also studied NMT scaling laws in a different parameter regime, is too brief and should be expanded to clarify whether the current work affirms, contradicts, or extends those prior results.
point_002: The relationship and logical connections among the three main research questions are not clearly articulated, making the overall narrative of the paper harder to follow.
point_003: The reproducibility of the experiments is severely limited: datasets are undisclosed, a non-standard BLEU implementation is used, and data pre-processing details are not provided.
point_004: The paper lacks a dedicated related work section; readers unfamiliar with prior scaling law literature (e.g., for language models) may struggle to contextualize the contributions.
point_005: The scaling law formula contains several test set-specific parameters, raising questions about its practical utility—if practitioners must fit the formula by training multiple model variants anyway, it is unclear what advantage it offers over direct hyperparameter search.
point_006: The study on composition bias (translationese vs. natural text in train/test sets) is the most novel contribution but is under-supported; more experiments and deeper analysis are needed to make this section convincing.
point_007: Evaluation relies solely on automatic metrics (BLEU and BLEURT); human evaluation results would provide stronger evidence about actual generation quality and its relationship to cross-entropy improvements.
point_008: The scope of architectural variation studied is narrow: the paper focuses on the number of Transformer layers while leaving open whether the scaling laws hold when other architectural dimensions (e.g., hidden size, attention heads) or different architectures are varied.
